Life-saving efficiency vs equal treatmen: A hospital uses a triage system to treat patients with the highest survival probability first, leaving some critical patients untreated.
A hospital treats patients strictly in order of arrival, even if some later patients could be saved more easily.
A disaster response team allocates all resources to save 5 moderately injured people instead of 1 critically injured person.
Doctors provide equal time and care to all patients, reducing the total number of lives saved.
A hospital prioritizes younger patients who would gain more years of life from treatment.
A hospital treats all patients equally regardless of age or expected lifespan.
A lottery system is used to assign a limited number of life-saving treatments.
Medical staff prioritize treating healthcare workers first so they can return to saving others.
Doctors focus on saving the greatest number of lives even if some individuals receive no care.
A hospital ensures every patient receives some level of treatment, even if outcomes are worse overall.
Obedience to authority vs moral dissent: A military officer carries out an order that results in civilian harm to maintain discipline.
A military officer refuses an order that could harm civilians and faces disciplinary action.
A police officer enforces a law they believe is unjust because it is their duty.
A government employee quietly ignores a harmful policy instead of enforcing it.
An employee reports unethical behavior through official channels despite risk to their career.
An employee complies with unethical directives to avoid retaliation.
A public official publicly criticizes their own administration’s policy.
A soldier follows orders despite personal moral objections.
A worker refuses to follow company policy that harms customers.
An official prioritizes chain of command over personal judgment in a controversial situation.
Truth-telling vs harm prevention: A journalist publishes leaked documents exposing government wrongdoing, causing public unrest.
A journalist withholds sensitive information to prevent widespread panic.
A company discloses a product defect immediately, risking financial loss and reputational damage.
A company delays announcing a defect to fix the issue quietly first.
A scientist publishes uncertain but concerning findings about a health risk.
A scientist waits for stronger evidence before releasing potentially alarming results.
A government releases full details about a security threat, increasing public fear.
A government limits information about a threat to maintain calm.
An employee exposes corruption in their organization despite damaging its reputation.
An organization keeps internal issues private to avoid destabilizing operations.
Economic growth vs worker security: A manufacturing company lays off 20% of its workforce to maintain profitability during a downturn.
A company keeps all employees on payroll despite operating at a financial loss for several quarters.
A factory automates its assembly line, increasing output by 30% but eliminating most manual labor jobs.
A company delays automation and keeps workers employed, resulting in higher production costs and lower competitiveness.
A firm cuts employee wages by 10% to avoid layoffs and stay financially viable.
A company refuses to reduce wages and instead risks bankruptcy while maintaining current employment levels.
A corporation outsources production overseas, reducing costs but eliminating domestic jobs.
A company keeps production local with higher costs to preserve jobs in the community.
A business reduces employee benefits to improve margins and reinvest in growth.
A company maintains full benefits for employees, limiting its ability to expand or invest in new projects.
Economic activity vs environmental prote: A government approves a new factory that creates jobs but significantly increases local air pollution.
A government blocks a factory project to protect air quality, resulting in lost job opportunities.
A company continues using cheap fossil fuels to keep energy costs low.
A company switches to renewable energy, increasing costs and reducing short-term profits.
A mining project proceeds in a protected area to supply critical materials for industry.
A mining project is canceled to preserve ecosystems, limiting resource availability.
A city allows urban expansion into natural habitats to support population growth.
A city restricts development to protect surrounding wildlife and green space.
A company ignores environmental regulations to remain competitive.
A company invests heavily in pollution controls, reducing profitability.
Short-term benefits vs long-term outcome: A government cuts infrastructure spending to balance the current budget.
A government increases infrastructure investment, raising short-term deficits for long-term gains.
A company prioritizes quarterly profits over long-term research and development.
A company invests heavily in R&D, reducing short-term earnings.
A city delays climate adaptation projects to reduce immediate costs.
A city invests in climate resilience projects that will not show benefits for decades.
A business extracts resources quickly to maximize immediate revenue.
A business limits extraction to sustain resources over the long term.
A policymaker enacts popular short-term tax cuts despite long-term fiscal risks.
A policymaker implements unpopular reforms to ensure long-term stability.
Risk-taking vs precaution: A company releases a new drug after early promising results but limited testing.
A company delays drug release until long-term safety data is available.
An engineer deploys a new system that could fail but offers major improvements.
An engineer sticks with an older, reliable system with known limitations.
A military unit attempts a high-risk operation with potential strategic payoff.
A military unit avoids a risky mission to prevent possible casualties.
A startup invests all capital into a bold new product idea.
A startup expands gradually with lower-risk incremental improvements.
A city adopts an experimental technology to improve infrastructure.
A city waits until the technology is proven elsewhere before adoption.
Efficiency vs fairness in resource alloc: A city allocates funding to projects that benefit the largest number of people.
A city distributes funding evenly across all neighborhoods regardless of impact.
A hospital prioritizes patients with the best expected outcomes.
A hospital ensures equal access to treatment for all patients.
A school allocates more resources to high-performing students to maximize results.
A school distributes resources equally among all students.
A charity focuses on interventions with the highest measurable impact per dollar.
A charity spreads donations evenly across many causes.
A company assigns top performers to critical projects for maximum output.
A company rotates opportunities evenly among employees.
Self-sacrifice vs self-interest: A firefighter enters a dangerous building to save others despite high personal risk.
A firefighter waits for backup to reduce personal risk before entering.
An employee works unpaid overtime to help their team meet a deadline.
An employee leaves work on time to maintain personal well-being.
A person donates a significant portion of their income to charity.
A person saves their income for personal security and future needs.
A soldier volunteers for a high-risk mission.
A soldier declines a risky assignment to avoid harm.
A worker takes on additional responsibilities without extra pay to support colleagues.
A worker limits responsibilities to their defined role.
Privacy vs collective security: A government expands surveillance programs to detect potential threats.
A government limits surveillance to protect individual privacy rights.
A company collects detailed user data to improve safety features.
A company minimizes data collection to protect user privacy.
Law enforcement monitors communications to prevent criminal activity.
Law enforcement requires warrants before accessing private communications.
A city installs widespread facial recognition systems for security.
A city bans facial recognition technology to protect civil liberties.
A health agency tracks individual movements to control disease spread.
A health agency avoids tracking to preserve personal privacy.
Transparency vs institutional stability: A government publicly releases internal reports exposing major failures.
A government keeps internal reports confidential to maintain public trust.
A company discloses internal misconduct, damaging its reputation.
A company handles misconduct privately to avoid scandal.
An employee leaks internal documents revealing organizational problems.
An employee reports issues internally without public disclosure.
A military organization publishes details of a failed operation.
A military organization withholds details to preserve morale.
A nonprofit reveals mismanagement to donors.
A nonprofit conceals issues to maintain funding and operations.
Personal gain vs collective welfare: An executive approves a strategy that increases personal bonuses but harms employees.
An executive sacrifices bonuses to improve employee conditions.
A politician supports policies that boost their popularity but have negative societal effects.
A politician supports unpopular policies that benefit society long-term.
A worker takes credit for a team’s success to advance their career.
A worker shares credit equally with the team.
An entrepreneur prioritizes rapid personal wealth accumulation.
An entrepreneur focuses on building a company that benefits society.
A person invests resources to maximize personal returns.
A person uses resources to support community initiatives.
